alternatives: Hi, how are you today?
Hey, how are you today?

phrases.phrase: Good morning, it's nice to see you
phrases.grammar: Contraction tip: 'it's' = 'it is'. Never use 'its' (possessive) for this.
alternatives: Good morning, it's great to see you
Good morning, it's good to see you
Good morning, it's a pleasure to see you

translations.es: Buenos días, es un placer verte

phrases.phrase: Hi there, how's your day going?
phrases.grammar: 'How's' = 'How is'. Present continuous ('going') describes an ongoing state.
alternatives: Hey there, how's your day going?
Hello there, how's your day going?

phrases.phrase: Good afternoon, how have you been?
phrases.grammar: 'How have you been?' uses the present perfect to ask about a period up to now — not just this moment.

alternatives: Hi, how's everything with you?
Hello, how's everything with you?

phrases.grammar: Subject drop in casual speech: 'Morning, hope you're well' omits 'Good' and 'I' — common in informal greetings.
alternatives: Morning, I hope you're doing well

translations.es: Buenos días, espero que estés bien

phrases.phrase: Good evening, how's your day been?

translations.es: Buenas noches, ¿cómo ha estado tu día?

phrases.grammar: Idiomatic use: 'treat' here means 'to deal with / affect'. 'How is life treating you?' = 'Is life going well for you?'
alternatives: Hi, how's life treating you?
Hello, how's life treating you?

phrases.phrase: Hi, it's good to catch up with you
phrases.grammar: 'Catch up' (phrasal verb) = to exchange news after time apart. Common collocations: 'catch up with someone', 'catch up on news'.
alternatives: Hey, it's good to catch up with you
Hello, it's good to catch up with you

translations.es: Hola, es bueno ponerse al día contigo

phrases.phrase: Hello, how have you been lately?
alternatives: Hi, how have you been lately?
Hey, how have you been lately?

phrases.phrase: Hi there, hope you're having a good day
alternatives: Hey there, hope you're having a good day
Hello there, hope you're having a good day

translations.es: Hola, espero que estés teniendo un buen día

alternatives: Hi, what's new with you?
Hello, what's new with you?

phrases.phrase: Hi, how are you holding up?
phrases.grammar: 'Holding up' (phrasal verb) = coping, managing under pressure. Implies checking in with empathy.
alternatives: Hey, how are you holding up?
Hello, how are you holding up?

phrases.phrase: Hey there, how's your week been?
alternatives: Hi there, how's your week been?
Hello there, how's your week been?
Hi, hope you're doing fine
Hey, hope you're doing fine

phrases.phrase: Hi, it's been a while, how are you?
phrases.grammar: 'It's been a while' uses present perfect to connect the past to the present. Contrast: 'It was a while ago' (simple past — finished).
alternatives: Hey, it's been a while, how are you?
Hello, it's been a while, how are you?

translations.es: Hola, ha pasado un tiempo, ¿cómo estás?

phrases.phrase: Good afternoon, how's life treating you?

translations.es: Buenas tardes, ¿cómo te trata la vida?

phrases.phrase: Hey, nice to see you again, how have you been?
alternatives: Hi, nice to see you again, how have you been?
Hello, nice to see you again, how have you been?

translations.es: Oye, es bueno verte de nuevo, ¿cómo has estado?

phrases.grammar: Exclamatory sentence: 'It was great seeing you!' uses the gerund after 'seeing'. Compare: 'It was great to see you!' — both correct, gerund is slightly more casual.
alternatives: It was great to see you!
It was good seeing you!
It was wonderful seeing you!

phrases.grammar: 'Take care of yourself' uses a reflexive pronoun. 'Take care' alone is also common as a farewell.
alternatives: Take care!
Have a great day!
Have a nice day!
Have a good day!

phrases.grammar: 'See you around' implies you'll meet again without fixing a time. More casual than 'See you later' or 'See you soon'.

phrases.phrase: It was lovely to meet you
phrases.grammar: Note the difference: 'Nice to meet you' (first time) vs 'Nice to see you' (you've met before). Mixing them is a common mistake.
alternatives: It was nice to meet you
It was great to meet you
It was a pleasure to meet you

phrases.grammar: 'Don't be a stranger' is an idiomatic farewell meaning 'keep in touch / visit again'. Not literal.

phrases.phrase: We should do this again sometime
phrases.grammar: 'We should do this again' — modal 'should' here is a suggestion, not an obligation. Very common in social farewells.

phrases.phrase: It was a pleasure, hope to see you again
alternatives: It was a pleasure, I hope to see you again

translations.es: Fue un placer, espero verte de nuevo

phrases.phrase: Say hi to everyone for me
phrases.grammar: 'Say hi to everyone for me' — indirect greeting via a third person. 'Say hi', 'give my regards', 'send my love' vary in formality.
alternatives: Say hello to everyone for me

phrases.phrase: Take care and stay in touch
phrases.grammar: 'Stay in touch' (phrasal verb) = keep communicating. Synonyms: 'keep in touch', 'keep me posted'.
alternatives: Take care and keep in touch

phrases.phrase: It was so good catching up with you
phrases.grammar: 'Catch up' revisited: 'catching up' as a gerund after 'good'. Compare phrase 8: 'catch up with you'.
alternatives: It was so great catching up with you
It was good catching up with you

translations.es: Qué bueno fue ponernos al día

phrases.phrase: Nice to meet you, I'm glad we connected
alternatives: Nice to meet you, I'm glad we met

translations.es: Mucho gusto, me alegra que nos hayamos conocido

phrases.grammar: 'Could be better' is a polite understatement meaning 'I'm not great'. English often uses understatement to avoid being negative.
Present perfect 'I've had' links a past event to the present feeling. Contrast: 'I had a great day' (simple past, already over).
'I've had better days' is an idiomatic understatement = 'today was bad'. Very common polite complaint.
'So' as an intensifier: 'Why are you SO happy?' adds emphasis. It can also signal mild surprise.
'What's on your mind?' is an idiom meaning 'What are you thinking about / worried about?'

phrases.phrase: Is there anything I can do for you?
phrases.grammar: 'Is there anything I can do for you?' — formal offer of help. Informal version: 'Can I help you with anything?'
alternatives: Is there anything I can help you with?
Can I do anything for you?

translations.es: ¿Hay algo que pueda hacer por tí?

phrases.phrase: I'd like to take a moment to introduce myself — I'm the new account manager for your region.

translations.es: Me gustaría presentarme — soy el nuevo gerente de cuentas para su región.

phrases.phrase: It was a pleasure meeting you today — I'll follow up by email with the details we discussed.

translations.es: Fue un placer conocerle hoy — le enviaré un correo con los detalles que hablamos.

phrases.phrase: Please allow me to introduce Dr. Martínez, who will be leading the session this afternoon.

translations.es: Permítame presentarle al Dr. Martínez, quien dirigirá la sesión de esta tarde.

phrases.grammar: 'Please allow me to introduce' — formal third-party introduction used in professional settings

phrases.phrase: I wanted to reach out and offer my sincere condolences on your loss.

translations.es: Quería comunicarme para ofrecerle mis más sinceras condolencias por su pérdida.

phrases.phrase: On behalf of the entire team, welcome aboard — we're thrilled to have you with us.

translations.es: En nombre de todo el equipo, bienvenido — estamos encantados de tenerte con nosotros.

phrases.phrase: I trust this message finds you well — I'm writing to follow up on our conversation last week.

translations.es: Espero que se encuentre bien — le escribo para continuar con nuestra conversación de la semana pasada.

phrases.grammar: 'I trust this message finds you well' — formal epistolary opener used in professional correspondence

phrases.phrase: It would be my pleasure to introduce our keynote speaker, whose work has been instrumental in shaping this field.

translations.es: Sería un placer presentar a nuestra conferenciante principal, cuyo trabajo ha sido fundamental en este campo.

phrases.phrase: I don't believe we've been formally introduced — I'm here representing the European delegation.

translations.es: Creo que no nos han presentado formalmente — estoy aquí representando a la delegación europea.

phrases.grammar: 'I don't believe we've been formally introduced' — a polished way to initiate a first introduction in a formal setting

phrases.phrase: Allow me to convey, on behalf of the delegation, how delighted we are to be received so graciously.

translations.es: Permítame expresar, en nombre de la delegación, lo encantados que estamos de ser recibidos con tanta generosidad.

phrases.phrase: I appreciate you taking the time to meet with me — I know how demanding your schedule must be.

translations.es: Le agradezco que haya encontrado tiempo para reunirse conmigo — sé lo exigente que debe ser su agenda.

phrases.grammar: 'I appreciate you taking the time' — gerund after 'appreciate' expresses formal gratitude

phrases.phrase: It is with great honor that we welcome the distinguished delegates to this commemorative gathering.

translations.es: Es con gran honor que damos la bienvenida a los distinguidos delegados a esta reunión conmemorativa.

phrases.grammar: 'It is with great honor that' — cleft construction used in ceremonial or formal addresses

phrases.phrase: Cultural awareness in greetings is not merely etiquette — it signals respect for the values of those you engage with.

translations.es: La conciencia cultural en los saludos no es solo protocolo — es una señal de respeto por los valores de quienes interactúas.

phrases.phrase: The protocol for introductions in this context requires that the senior representative speak first.

translations.es: El protocolo de presentaciones en este contexto exige que el representante de mayor rango hable primero.

phrases.grammar: 'requires that … speak' — formal subjunctive after verbs of requirement or mandate

phrases.phrase: I would be remiss not to acknowledge the immense contribution you have made to this institution over the years.

translations.es: Sería una negligencia de mi parte no reconocer la inmensa contribución que ha aportado a esta institución a lo largo de los años.

phrases.grammar: 'I would be remiss not to' — formal expression of obligation to acknowledge or address something

phrases.phrase: Please accept my most sincere felicitations on the occasion of your well-deserved promotion.

translations.es: Por favor, acepte mis más sinceras felicitaciones con motivo de su tan merecido ascenso.
